title: CHAPTER I.
summary: A little girl named Alice went into a dark hall and couldn't get out. She found a golden key on a table, but it wouldn't open any of the doors. She then found a bottle with the words “DRINK ME” written on it. After a while, she decided to go into the garden, but forgot the key to the door.

title: CHAPTER II.
summary: The Pool of Tears is the story of Alice's Right Foot, Esq., Hearthrug, near the Fender. It is the tale of a little girl who loses her feet at Christmas. She cries for her feet to be put in shoes and stockings. The Rabbit returns with a pair of white kid gloves and a large fan for her.

title: CHAPTER III.
summary: A Caucus-Race and a Long Tale by William Makepeace Thackeray. Makepeace tells the story of William the Conqueror. The story is told through the eyes of Alice, a young woman. The tale is told in the form of a short story. It is written in the style of a novel.

title: CHAPTER IV.
summary: The White Rabbit was looking for the fan and the pair of white kidgloves. Alice was so much frightened that she ran off in the direction it pointed to. She found a neat little house, on the door of which was a bright brassplate with the name “W. RABBIT,” engraved upon it.

title: CHAPTER V.
summary: Alice was walking through a wood when a large pigeon flew into her face. The Pigeon screamed, “Serpent!” and beat her with its wings. “I’m a little girl,” said Alice, rather doubtfully, as she remembered the number of changes she had gone through.

title: CHAPTER VI.
summary: Pig and Pepper is the story of a young girl and her encounter with a house. Alice was carrying a baby pig when she saw the Cheshire Cat. The Cat said that the Hatter was mad, and the Hare was mad too. Alice asked the Cat how it knew she was mad and the Cat replied: “We’re all mad here. I’m mad”

title: CHAPTER VII.
summary: A Mad Tea-Party is a story about a Mad Tea Party. The Hatter, the March Hare and the Dormouse were having tea at a table under a tree in front of the house. The three were all crowded together at one corner of the table; “No room! No room!” they cried out when they saw Alice.

title: CHAPTER VIII.
summary: The Queen’s Croquet-Ground is described by Alice in her story. The story is told in the form of a narrative poem. The poem is called ‘The Queen and the White Rabbit’ and is published by William Makepeace Thackeray, London. Alice, a young girl, was in a procession with the Queen of England. The Queen was holding a game of croquet.

title: CHAPTER IX.
summary: The Mock Turtle’s Story is about a young woman and a flamingo. The story is told by Alice and the Mock Turtle, who meet in the kitchen of the Duchess. The Mock Turtle is the story of a young girl and a young boy. The Queen took Alice to see the Mockurtle, and to hear his history.

title: CHAPTER X.
summary: The Lobster Quadrille is a very pretty dance,’ said Alice timidly. “Would you like to see a little of it?” said the Mock Turtle. Gryphon and Mock Turtle asked Alice to tell them about her adventures. Alice was a little nervous about it just at first, but gained courage as she went on.

title: CHAPTER XI.
summary: Alice had never been in a court of justice before, but she had read about them in books. She was quite pleased to find that she knew the name of nearly everything there. The jury eagerly wrote down all three dates on their slates, and then added them up. Alice felt a curious sensation, which puzzled her a good deal until she made out what it was.

title: CHAPTER XII.
summary: Alice’s Evidence is about the trial of the Lizard in the court of the King. The jury were asked to write out a history of the trial. “I seem to see some meaning in them, after all,’ said the King, spreading out the verses on his knee, and looking atthem with one eye.
